content:
2 min read
7 hours ago

--

A few months ago I was deep in placement prep, doing what every engineering student does: opening LeetCode, picking a problem, staring at it, and either solving it or copying a solution I didn’t really understand.
The problem wasn’t effort. It was that I could solve a problem and still have no real intuition for what was happening inside it. I’d memorize a pattern without seeing it. Two-pointer, sliding window, DP transitions — I could recite the idea but couldn’t picture it moving.
So I built the tool I actually wanted: DheetCode — a DSA visualizer covering 150+ LeetCode-style problems, where you can actually see the algorithm work through the data instead of just reading a Java solution and hoping it clicks.
What it is
DheetCode walks through each problem step by step, visually — arrays shifting, pointers moving, recursion trees expanding — so the pattern behind the problem becomes obvious instead of memorized. It’s built with Next.js, TypeScript, and Tailwind, and it’s free to use.
Why I built it this way
I’m an Electronics and Telecommunications engineering student, not a CS student — placement prep at my college doesn’t even really support students from my branch, so a lot of this was self-taught, off-campus, figured out the hard way. That background made one thing obvious to me: most DSA resources assume you already have the intuition. They give you the answer, not the *seeing*. I wanted the tool that would’ve saved me the six months I spent confused.
The part I’m not proud of
I launched this at the end of May. Then, on May 31st, I had surgery. Recovery took over a month. When I came back, I had a live product, a Reddit post, two LinkedIn posts, and about 136 organic users who found it with almost zero promotion — a real signal that this was solving something for people.
And then I just… stopped posting. Not because it didn’t work. Because it did, and something about showing up consistently and asking people to look at what I built is harder than building it in the first place. That’s on me, and I’m done sitting on it.
Where it’s going
- A Chrome extension is built, just not promoted yet
- A contest section is planned, with sandboxed code execution for real-time practice
But right now, the priority is simple: get it in front of people actually prepping for SDE interviews, and find out what’s missing.
If you’re grinding DSA and tired of solving things you don’t really see— try it: dheetcode.in
Feedback, brutal or otherwise, is welcome. That’s kind of the whole point of finally posting this.
